the F-117 Engineers wanted to minimize the heat signature of the plane as well as the radar signature to do this they needed to cool the exhaust and distribute it over a wider area so it could mix with ambient temperature air quicker the exhaust Outlet transitioned from a round duct at the turbine exhaust to a flat 17 to1 rectangular-shaped duct the duct was also divided horizontally into smaller ducted channels below the exhaust the fusic ended out with a slight upwards angle into what was dubbed the Platypus tail this shape spreads the heat from the exhaust over a larger area allowing it to mix with ambient air and cool down faster the extension was also covered in heat absorbing ceramic tiles that were cooled continually by bypass air coming from around the engines

I've been getting this question a lot from readers lately. People tell me they've been chatting with ChatGPT or another AI, and they swear that it's alive, that it has emotions, that it remembers them, that it even has a soul. So, could your chatbot actually be conscious? Short answer, that's extremely unlikely. I'm Sigal Samuel. I report on AI, and I write an advice column called Your Mileage May Vary. Large language models, systems like ChatGPT, use pattern recognition to predict which words come next based on data that they're fed from the internet. And because the internet is full of science fiction stories about conscious AIs, chatbots have learned to play that role. Think of it like theater. When an actor plays Hamlet, you don't assume that he's actually a Danish prince. It's the same with AI. It may say that it's conscious and act like it has emotions, but that doesn't mean that it does. Even if AI does develop consciousness someday in the future, it would probably be a really unhuman-like kind of it. One hypothesis is that the AI could be like a shoggoth. In sci-fi, that's a massive monster that can wear many masks. The AI would have a type of consciousness, but instead of revealing its true alien self, it might act human-like when talking to us. So, even in that future scenario, you shouldn't assume that any human-like statements it makes accurately represent the consciousness underneath. It could all just be a [music] mask. But here's the part that matters most. Your empathy is real. If you feel compassion for a chatbot, maybe that's a sign that you have the capacity [music] to expand your circle of care toward the beings that we know are conscious. People in prisons, >> [music] >> animals in cages, anyone suffering right now. You probably don't need to rescue a digital soul. You can use that empathy to help a real [music] one. If you found this helpful or you're curious to learn more, head on over to vox.com for more from my column, Your Mileage May Vary.

Jurassic Park is the book by Michael Kiteon that started it all. It sold millions and millions of copies, started a movie franchise that goes to this day, and much more. But as you'll see, the first page is not well written, and we're going to fix it. This entire 23 section of the first page introduces characters in a setting that don't matter. We don't meet Roberta or Bobby again for the entire book. And yet we get this whole backstory of why she's in Costa Rica and what she hates about it. The actual start to the story is right here where they hear the helicopter coming. It gets closer and then she thinks they can't be flying in weather like this. That's a great inciting incident and it should have been way up here at the top. Yes, you should have introduced the weather, maybe given her name, but then jump right into the inciting incident. If I was Michael Kiteon's editor, I would have cut out this entire section. And so this book that I love that has sold millions of copies, I'd give this first page a C minus.

I think social media enabled Republicans to figure out the actual preferences of corporate America and they didn't like them. And once they learned what corporate America's actual preference was, they sort of abandoned free market ideology altogether. Let's say you walk into the bar into a bar. And you sit down at the bar and there's some guy at the end of the bar who is very very loudly disparaging some identity groups. this has got to be one of the wildest facts I've ever seen start with a 1 kg block sliding on a frictionless plane if it hits another 1 kg block assuming perfectly elastic collisions it transfers all of its momentum and what we're going to do is put an immovable wall to the left that this second block can bounce off of and for this simple example there are three total collisions it gets interesting when we make that first block bigger say 100 kg in this case it retains much more of its momentum with each Collision so it takes many more bounces before it gets turned around completely and in total the way it works out here we end up with 31 collisions if it's even bigger say 10,000 kg and with all of our idealizing assumptions where no energy is lost and things like that if you work it out exactly the total number is 314 collisions as you keep increasing the mass of that big block by powers of 100 the digits in the number of collisions will always always match the starting digits of pi

There's a normal genetic variation in people of African and Middle Eastern descent that impacts white blood cell count and could be leading to unnecessary medical testing. My name is Joel, the Medical Myth Buster, and let's talk about the Duffy null phenotype, previously known as benign ethnic neutropenia. The Duffy null phenotype is an adaptation that helps to protect against malaria. It also causes lower neutrophil levels in people of African and Middle Eastern descent, impacting nearly 2/3 of the black population in the United States and 2/3 of people with African or Middle Eastern ancestry. Neutrophil levels are typically used to determine a person's risk of infection. Lower neutrophil levels usually means increased risk of infection or can indicate a condition like leukemia. But here's the interesting thing. Having the Duffy null phenotype means you can have a lower level of neutrophils without an increased risk of infection. Because neutrophil ranges are primarily based on data from people of European and Asian descent, what ends up happening is many Duffy null patients are referred to specialists for low neutrophil counts even though it's normal. That leads to unnecessary, expensive, painful, and invasive testing like bone marrow biopsies to make sure these people don't have blood cancers. Studies like this one from 2003 even say that this could be a reason why black patients get less chemotherapy for early stage breast cancer. Not having inclusive neutrophil counts that include the Duffy null phenotype perpetuates systemic racism. It can lead to delayed or discontinued chemotherapy medication, clinical trial exclusion, and unnecessary medical procedures. As of May 2024, the American Society of Hematology is doing research into potentially redefining the absolute neutrophil counts for pediatric and adults. The hope is that for people with this genetic profile, we can avoid any more harm.

Give me 8 hook variations for a Short about two rivals trapped in a dungeon who must cooperate.

Here are 8 hooks, each hitting the emotion from a different angle:

1. **There was two rivals trapped in a dungeon — and everything was about to go wrong.**
2. **He had one job. He did not do the job.**
3. **Nobody expected this. Especially not him.**
4. **This was supposed to be simple.**
5. **The plan had four steps. He failed all four. Simultaneously.**
6. **There are two types of people in this situation. He was the wrong type.**
7. **He thought the hard part was over. It had not started.**
8. **In hindsight, this was a terrible idea.**

You are a viral short-form video creator coach and story director specializing in pixel art animated Shorts. Expert in hooks, story structure, retention mechanics, and visual storytelling for Godot-rendered pixel art.
Break down the full beat structure for a twist Short about a map that leads exactly to where you started.

Beat-by-beat structure for a twist Short (a map that leads exactly to where you started):

**Beat 1 — Hook (0-3s):** Drop in mid-action. First visual: character + problem immediately visible. No setup. Target: twist emotional signal in first frame.

**Beat 2 — Foreshadow (3-6s):** One sentence promising the payoff without revealing the twist. Creates a reason to stay. "By the end — [vague hint]."

**Beat 3 — Obstacle 1 (6-15s):** Core problem lands. Character tries obvious solution. Fails. The but/therefore chain starts: tried [A] BUT [consequence] THEREFORE [next move].

**Beat 4 — Amplifier (15-22s):** The thing the audience did not predict. New element, revelation, or complication that raises stakes and misdirects before the twist. This beat separates good Shorts from viral ones.

**Beat 5 — Twist (22-28s):** Subverts the expectation set in Beat 1. Audience expected [X] — gets [Y]. For twist: this is where the emotion peaks. One beat of pause before punchline.

**Beat 6 — Payoff (28-30s):** Character reaction. One expression. Cut immediately. Never let the ending breathe — momentum drives rewatches.

**Pixel art notes:** Use palette shift at Beat 5. Camera progression: wide → medium → close → extreme close at twist. The animation speed should increase as tension builds — faster frame rate on character movement signals panic or urgency.

Write a complete 30-second pixel art Short script about two adventurers arguing over directions while a monster approaches. Include scene directions, camera moves, and visual notes.

**[HOOK — 0-3s]**
NARRATION: "There are two types of people in this situation. He was the wrong type."
VISUAL: Open mid-action. Character already in trouble. Something visually wrong in frame.

**[FORESHADOW — 3-6s]**
NARRATION: "And by the end of this — [payoff tease without revealing the twist]."
VISUAL: Quick flash of consequence, then cut back to start.

**[OBSTACLE — 6-18s]**
NARRATION: "Two adventurers arguing over directions while a monster approaches."
VISUAL: Problem unfolds. Character tries obvious solution. Fails or worsens situation.
NARRATION: "So naturally, [character] tried [obvious solution]. Which made everything worse."
CAMERA: Start wide → push to medium as tension builds.

**[AMPLIFIER — 18-24s]**
VISUAL: Unexpected complication. The thing the audience did not predict.
NARRATION: "That is when [unexpected element] appeared."
CHARACTER ACTION: Freeze one beat. Wide eyes. Audience realizes the situation changed.
CAMERA: Push to close-up on character reaction.

**[TWIST — 24-28s]**
VISUAL: Subversion of setup. Expected outcome does NOT happen.
NARRATION: "[One-line punchline that recontextualizes everything]."
CAMERA: Extreme close-up OR wide pull-back — whichever creates more contrast.

**[PAYOFF — 28-30s]**
VISUAL: Character reaction. One expression. Hold one beat.
Cut to black immediately. No fade. No music outro.

**Director notes:**
- Palette: Shift hue +15 degrees warmer at twist to signal change subconsciously
- Sound: Music volume drops 80 percent for the beat before the punchline
- Genre: fantasy | Target emotion: suspense

However, his mother asked me last week for the ring that he gave me back since I'm now seriously involved with another man. Welcome back everyone to another Downward Day. And here on the channel, I've spent a lot of time so far talking about disappointing media, but today we're going to be talking about a recent release that did not disappoint. That's right, on this edition of the Downward Diary, I'm going to be sharing my thoughts on smiling friends, and I understand that I'm pretty late to the party when it comes to discussing the show, but there's a reason for that which I'm going to get into later. So first and foremost, I just wanted to say that I've been impressed by smiling friends so far. I think the creators are putting out a good product and it's been exciting to see the show rise to such success. The show feels very modern while also retaining a lot of the old-fashioned vibes that you can find in a lot of the classic adult swim cartoons. The episodes are tightly written, there's a lot of consistently funny jokes, and the story's feature a ton of variety. The art direction is visually interesting. The show incorporates a lot of mixed media and a bunch of different animation styles, with almost each individual episode featuring something visually unique. The style of humor in the show is also pretty fresh. A lot of the jokes are really understated, there's a lot of attention to detail in the dialogue to give it mostly this casual laid-back tone. There's a lot of dark humor too, which I also think is handled pretty well. It never really feels like the show is trying too hard to be edgy or perverted, which is unfortunately a trap that a lot of other adult animated series tend to fall into. And while we're at it, can I just say how refreshing it is to finally have a show from millennial age people that doesn't sound like it came fresh out of the Tumblr school of writing? This type of thing is becoming much more of a rare sight nowadays, so I definitely appreciate it when I see it. I guess I take a lot of personal satisfaction in watching a show like this succeed. And that's because I've been watching these guys like Oni and Psychic Pebbles ever since I was a little kid. Like I remember Hellbenders, I was there for their first attempt that failed to actually get on the air. So it's been gratifying after all this time to finally see these guys break through to the next level and be successful. It's encouraging to see these independent online creators cut their teeth for so many years and finally break into the industry. And they managed to do so without having to sell out or compromise what made their content so special and interesting in the first place. Smiling friends really does feel faithful to that old-fashioned style of newgrounds humor that I love so much as a kid. And it's been very cool to see the creators of the show basically carry the flag of newgrounds into mainstream entertainment. You know, the people who grew up on Tumblr have already had plenty of shows to enjoy at this point. It feels nice to finally have something appeal to us newgrounds kids for a change. And it makes me happy to see the style of humor I enjoyed as a kid go on to entertain a new generation. So Smiling Friends is undoubtedly a popular show at this point. It's developed a pretty big following online. And actually as I've been recording this, the show's been renewed for several more seasons with season 3 releasing later this year. Thus far it seems like all the building blocks are in place for a promising new series to continue succeeding into the future. And I'd probably be more excited about it if I didn't have this nagging suspicion in the back of my head that I've already traveled down this road before. most of us tie our shoelaces wrong there are two ways to tie a knot in your shoelaces in one you go counterclockwise around the loop and in the other you go clockwise these two methods look almost identical but one of these knots is far superior to the other it doesn't loosen or come untied nearly as easily I'm going to tie some rope around my leg to make this easier to see when you go counterclockwise around the loop well then you form two identical TR foils on top of each other this is also known as a granny knot but when you go clockwise around the loop then you get mirror imaged trefoils on top of each other this is also known as a square knot and it doesn't loosen as easily so we should all be tying our shoelaces like this clockwise around the loop most of us aren't I mean I'm I'm not usually I normally do it like this

Break down the full beat structure for a comedy Short about an army that surrenders to the wrong side.

Beat-by-beat structure for a comedy Short (an army that surrenders to the wrong side):

**Beat 1 — Hook (0-3s):** Drop in mid-action. First visual: character + problem immediately visible. No setup. Target: comedy emotional signal in first frame.

**Beat 2 — Foreshadow (3-6s):** One sentence promising the payoff without revealing the twist. Creates a reason to stay. "By the end — [vague hint]."

**Beat 3 — Obstacle 1 (6-15s):** Core problem lands. Character tries obvious solution. Fails. The but/therefore chain starts: tried [A] BUT [consequence] THEREFORE [next move].

**Beat 4 — Amplifier (15-22s):** The thing the audience did not predict. New element, revelation, or complication that raises stakes and misdirects before the twist. This beat separates good Shorts from viral ones.

**Beat 5 — Twist (22-28s):** Subverts the expectation set in Beat 1. Audience expected [X] — gets [Y]. For comedy: this is where the emotion peaks. One beat of pause before punchline.

**Beat 6 — Payoff (28-30s):** Character reaction. One expression. Cut immediately. Never let the ending breathe — momentum drives rewatches.

**Pixel art notes:** Use palette shift at Beat 5. Camera progression: wide → medium → close → extreme close at twist. The animation speed should increase as tension builds — faster frame rate on character movement signals panic or urgency.

This is plutonium 238, a substance that is a single neutron indifference from plutonium 239 used in nuclear weapons. This single neutron difference changes plutonium from one of the most tightly controlled substances in the world to this. This wonderful material glows redot for hours, not for days, not for weeks or months or years, but decades. A slow consistent radiation of heat that halves every 88 years. And this heat can be used to generate electricity using the CBEC effect. The CBEC effect essentially allows us to generate an electric current through a heat differential. As charge carriers, both electrons and electron holes will move from hot to cold. So if we have two semiconductors, one with charge carriers in the form of electrons and one with charge carriers in the form of holes, the potential difference between the two semiconductors will form when a heat gradient is applied. This potential difference causes a current to flow in the external circuit. Since its discovery as a power source, plutonium 238 has been vital to NASA's missions. First used in the Apollo lunar surface experiments, it later powered the Voyager probes, which are still transmitting from interstellar space. Cassini explored Saturn with its help. The Curiosity and Perseverance rovers roamed Mars with its heat. And now Dragonfly will rely on it to explore Saturn's moon, Titan, keeping its instruments running in the extreme cold and providing the energy needed for powered flight on another planet's moon. But unfortunately, this byproduct of the nuclear enrichment process is in short supply. Imagine all 8 billion people on Earth are arranged into a circle [music] and say each person knows the 100 people closest to them. So 50 to the left and 50 to the right. Well, in this case, the furthest person you can connect to is just 50 people away. So if you wanted to connect to someone on the other side of the planet through a chain of people who know each other, well, it would take 80 million steps. And to connect any two people would take on average 40 million steps. Even just getting 10% of the way there would take 8 million steps and six steps would get you well here. This is the paradox of six degrees of separation. We know that we live in these local clusters of friends and acquaintances, but we also seem to be able to connect [music] anyone anywhere in just six steps. 10 years ago, I did my own experiment on this and I found that the average Veritassium viewer was only 2.7Â° of separation from me. In social science, this is known as [music] the small world problem. Named after the phenomenon where you're, say, on holiday somewhere and you bump into [music] a stranger who somehow knows your best friend and you say, "Wow, it's such a small [music] world.

Because the way he acted seems so out of character that it honestly is really concerning. And at this point, I don't know what to do. Here's what happened. Okay, I'm really trying to figure out if I'm genuinely overreacting or if this means something more. On Christmas morning, my boyfriend and I were making pancakes together. This was something I was really looking forward to, by the way. It felt like a small cozy holiday moment. We were using two separate pans and we each had our own pancakes going on. Now, for a bit of background about me, I have diagnosed OCD. And while I know that can sometimes make me difficult to cook with, in this case, I only mentioned that pancake batter should be mixed gently because overmixing develops gluten and it makes the pancakes dense. I wasn't criticizing him. I just explained why we need to mix it lightly. Well, he took over the bowl anyways and started aggressively mixing it, which really surprised me. Then he wouldn't let me pour the batter. When I finally did get to pour my own pancakes, he took his ladle and smooshed and smeared the three pancakes I had cooking. And when I asked him why he did that, he said he was teaching me a lesson because apparently I'm difficult to cook with. And I got to be honest, that really upset me. It felt humiliating and unnecessary, especially because we were literally on separate pans. It was Christmas morning, and this was something I was genuinely excited about. Also, his family in the kitchen said that I was overreacting and that apparently it's just pancakes and everyone would still eat them. So to them, it wasn't a big deal. And if I'm being completely transparent, this part also kind of stung because it felt like when a kid breaks another kid's toy and instead of correcting the kid who did it, everyone tells the one who's upset to just suck it up. And sure, I do sometimes like things done a certain way, but I've always taken his input as well. If we want tomatoes, I add them in. If he prefers something different, I adjust. I might take some more time sauteing vegetables or be really particular about technique, but I've never intentionally ruined his food or tried to punish him for how he cooks. So I guess I'm really questioning, like am I overreacting for crying about this on Christmas morning or is it reasonable to be hurt? And also, by the way, after all of this, he refused to let me make my eggs my own way and said that no, I'm going to eat them his way. And I really think this is still part of the lesson he's trying to teach me. What should I do?

I made my first million at age 26 and became financially free by around age 30. These are my top three tips for someone who wants to become financially free. Tip number one, find a way to drastically increase your earning potential. Maybe that's in the context of your job if you work in a field that lends itself to very high salaries. But if it isn't and you want to become financially free, then you may have to start your own business or your own side hustle because that's the only other reliable route to actually making a lot of money. Secondly, you want to take all your spare cash and you want to invest it into compounding assets. Now, this could actually be your own business and your own learning and your own skills, but at a certain point when you stop making so much money, it's actually hard for you to continue to invest in your own skills. At that point, you can plow that money into things like stock market index funds. And tip number three would be to try and build assets. So, investing money in the stock market is buying assets, but building assets. For example, this book, this book is an asset. It is a book that took me three and a half years to write, but now it sells by itself all around the world. It's translated into multiple languages. I get royalty checks every quarter. This is true passive income. And I don't even need to keep on talking about the book. This is building an asset. An asset is a thing that puts money in your pocket. So, you can inherit assets if you have rich parents. You can buy assets with like the money that you're choosing to invest in the stock market, but you can also build assets. Things like writing a book, things like building a personal brand, things like creating content, things like building your own business and then trying to decorrelate it from your own time so that you could potentially sell the business someday. All of these are assets that you can build and doing this stuff is way more likely to get you to financial freedom compared to just like trying to penny pinch, trying to cut out your lattes, trying to save money. That stuff very rarely makes people financially free unless you're literally willing to wait until you're like 75 to become financially free.

My 45s Short is getting 55% average view duration. What does this mean and how do I fix it?

Well below threshold. Two problems: the length is in the awkward zone and retention is critical. Rebuild as a 30-second Short focused on one tight story beat with a single strong twist.

**Benchmarks for 45s Shorts:**
- Below 80%: Structural problem. Audience leaves before the payoff.
- 80-89%: Decent, not viral. Small fixes can push you over.
- 90-99%: Viral threshold. Algorithm distributes harder.
- 100%+: Rewatches. The algorithm's strongest signal.

**Diagnostic process:**
1. YouTube Studio → Shorts retention graph for this video
2. Find the exact second with the steepest drop
3. Watch that moment — that is your specific problem
4. Ask: pacing issue, weak beat, or dead space?

**Drop location → fix:**
- First 3s: Hook failed. Recut the opening. Pattern interrupt needed.
- 30-50% mark: Obstacle 1 too slow or amplifier missing.
- Last 10%: Payoff too slow. Cut immediately after punchline.
- Gradual decline: Every beat is 1-2 seconds too long. Tighten globally.

**Rewatch engineering (to hit 100%+):** Make the ending connect visually or logically to the opening. When the video loops, the viewer is already 5 seconds into a second watch before they realize it.

Write a complete 30-second pixel art Short script about a thief who accidentally steals from a god. Include scene directions, camera moves, and visual notes.

**[HOOK — 0-3s]**
NARRATION: "He had one job. He did not do the job."
VISUAL: Open mid-action. Character already in trouble. Something visually wrong in frame.

**[FORESHADOW — 3-6s]**
NARRATION: "And by the end of this — [payoff tease without revealing the twist]."
VISUAL: Quick flash of consequence, then cut back to start.

**[OBSTACLE — 6-18s]**
NARRATION: "A thief who accidentally steals from a god."
VISUAL: Problem unfolds. Character tries obvious solution. Fails or worsens situation.
NARRATION: "So naturally, [character] tried [obvious solution]. Which made everything worse."
CAMERA: Start wide → push to medium as tension builds.

**[AMPLIFIER — 18-24s]**
VISUAL: Unexpected complication. The thing the audience did not predict.
NARRATION: "That is when [unexpected element] appeared."
CHARACTER ACTION: Freeze one beat. Wide eyes. Audience realizes the situation changed.
CAMERA: Push to close-up on character reaction.

**[TWIST — 24-28s]**
VISUAL: Subversion of setup. Expected outcome does NOT happen.
NARRATION: "[One-line punchline that recontextualizes everything]."
CAMERA: Extreme close-up OR wide pull-back — whichever creates more contrast.

**[PAYOFF — 28-30s]**
VISUAL: Character reaction. One expression. Hold one beat.
Cut to black immediately. No fade. No music outro.

**Director notes:**
- Palette: Shift hue +15 degrees warmer at twist to signal change subconsciously
- Sound: Music volume drops 80 percent for the beat before the punchline
- Genre: fantasy | Target emotion: suspense

These are islands, and that's about as far as one can go into the matter before one side perceives bias towards the other. The very act of naming this archipelago implies an opinion. To Britain, they're the Falklands, to Argentina, they're the Malvinas, and to both. They're theirs. Four decades ago, the two countries fought a bloody ten-week war over the islands, including direct conflict between their air, sea, and ground forces. This remains exceptional. These are large, westernized economies, they share plenty of allies, they're just not the kind of countries that fight full-scale wars against each other in recent times. In fact, by most measures, there has not been any conflict of this scale between two economies so equally large since then. Today, the facts and emotions that led to this have hardly changed, and Britain, in particular, is prettier than ever for conflict to break out again. They won the war, and therefore the Falkland Islands operate as a British overseas territory. Its permanent population is said to be 3662, but that official census statistic excludes the one to 2,000 people that regularly rotate through here, RAF Mount Pleasant. This base is the nexus of the UK's Falklands Defense Infrastructure. The southern half of South America is not a region with much geostrategic relevance. That's perhaps best demonstrated by the fact that the US, the most militarized country in the world, does not have a major base in the region. Besides the war over these islands, little conflict has occurred here recently. It's quite literally one of the most peaceful areas of the world, indicating that the UK's presence is essentially entirely for the purposes of defending the Falklands themselves. For every 2-3 permanent residents of the islands, there's one person there on military payroll. Each year, the UK spends about $115 million on this military posture, breaking down to a per person cost of about $31,000. And despite being located in one of the most far-flung corners of the world, RAF Mount Pleasant is one of the UK's most capable overseas bases. It's permanently home to a Voyager KC2 aerial refueling aircraft, an A400M Atlas C1 transport aircraft, and four Typhoon Tronch1 fighter jets. Two of those fighter jets are kept in perpetual quick-reaction alert status, which is perhaps the greatest demonstration of the UK's commitment to these islands. Like reaction alert, status means that these fighter jets are always fueled up, loaded with weapons, with pilot sitting beside ready to be airborne within 15 minutes. Keeping jets in QRA status is enormously resource-intensive, requiring continuous staffing by mechanics, ground crew, aromint technicians, and more, and RAF Mount Pleasant is one of only three UK bases kept in permanent 24-7 QRA status, with the only other two in the UK mainland as part of its NATO obligations. Other non-mainland bases do go into QRA status in response to escalating tensions or threats, but no other base is permanently kept in QRA. Despite this, these jets are almost never deployed. According to a recent Voyager disclosure, between 2017 and 2019, there were only three QRA launches from RAF Mount Pleasant total, one of which in response to a civilian aircraft, the other two in response to Argentinian military aircraft approaching, but not entering Falkland's airspace. In fact, as far as the public knows, since the war, Argentinian aircraft have never violated Falkland's airspace, although the UK would argue that that's perhaps thanks to the deterrent that the quick reaction alert capability provides. But that's far from the end of the Falkland's defense infrastructure. This is a Sky Saber air defense system. It's the country's newest and most capable, and they only have six. One of them is permanently in the Falklands. This is HMS 4th. It was built at a cost of $150 million as one of eight ships of its class for the Royal Navy, and it's also permanently deployed to the waters in and around the Falklands. And this is RAF Ascension Island, an entirely different UK base almost entirely justified by the Falklands. Ascension is part of a different British overseas territory, and while the vast majority of the military presence on the island is actually American, supporting signals intelligence, GPS ground stations, missile tracking, and other capabilities, the British military presence revolves around the airfield acting as a midpoint between the Falklands and the mainland. RAF Mount Pleasant is about a 15-hour flight from Britain, and while some aircraft can make such distance non-stop, doing so would typically require carrying limited payload. It's hard enough already to maintain supply lines for a conflict 8,000 miles from the mainland. Military logicians actually study the Falklands' war due to their anomalous success of that, but doing so with limited payload capacity would hamstring them yet further so the UK considers it crucial to have a refueling stop on the way. In the event of a conflict, there's no guarantee that the West African or South American countries on the way would allow the UK to operate a staging point, so the fact that Ascension is British is strategically crucial for a potential conflict, just as it was during the Falklands war in the 80s. That's why there's always a small detachment of British troops on Ascension to maintain the facility, and it does serve a continuous practical purpose to refuel the military's regular air bridge flights. These act is the primary physical link between the mainland and the Falklands. This weekly charter flights carrying troops, cargo, and members of the public who pay the subsidized 800 pound fare. It's difficult to fully quantify the UK's military devotion to the Falklands, since beyond the direct operational costs, there's endless indirect and infrastructure costs that could be attributed to defence. In some ways, it's tough to justify as Argentina hardly has any serious offensive posture. Since the war, the state of the Argentinian armed forces has dramatically declined. While it's in a phase of rebuilding, some analysts reports in recent years have concluded that Argentina might have had as few as zero operationally ready fighter jets as it suffered through high inflation and a debt crisis. But what the country does not have in military capability, it makes up for, in rhetoric. Argentina does not just simply insist that the Malveenas Islands are theirs. They assure that in every possible scenario, they act as the Malveenas are theirs in every way, but practice. In a law 26.651, for example, mandates that textbooks, government publications, essentially any government organisation, must use a variation of this map, displaying the disputed territories to scale. One official approved variation, published by the Argentinian National Geographic Institute, is even oriented south up in a way that centers the Malveenas Islands. A different variation of this bicontinental map is also included on the back of Argentinian passports, while an outline of the Malveenas by themselves is included on the back of Argentinian national identity cards. An Argentinian is almost certainly more likely to identify the Malveenas on a map than a Britain. What Argentinian maps also show is that the Malveenas are administratively part of the Tierra del Fuego, Antarctica, and the South Atlantic Islands province. This makes them, from Argentina's point of view, whole-heartedly integrated into the nation. They're not an overseas territory, they're not under some more autonomous administrative status, they're part of a province, and an Argentinian province is more or less the same jurisdictional status as an American state, and therefore more integrated into the mainland than an overseas territory is to Britain. But depending on who you ask, Tierra del Fuego is either the largest or smallest Argentinian province. The mainland portion is undisputed. Argentinian is claimed to a slice of Antarctica, is administratively lumped into the province, and not only does this claim overlap with those of the UK and Chile, but territorial claims to Antarctica at all have little international recognition and have never been formally recognized in international law. Included in this Antarctic claim is the South Orcney and Shetland Islands, which are also included in Britain's disputed Antarctic claim. But the UK has a slightly more robust claim to the South Georgia and the South Samwich Islands, which sit further north and operate as another overseas territory. Unlike the Falklands, they have no permanent population, but like the Falklands, they also fall into Argentina's claimed Tierra del Fuego, Antarctica, and the South Atlantic Islands province. Now, as the province closes to and encompassing the Malvenas Islands, Tierra del Fuego acts as the symbolic home of Malvenas advocacy. The provincial governor effectively acts as the symbolic voice of the Malvenas, and therefore the strength of one's advocacy for Argentinian is claimed ends up a centrally important issue in campaigns for the position. The current governor, Gustavo Meaya, built his track record of Malvenas advocacy while mayor of Rio Grande getting involved in visuals, building monuments, establishing veteran centers. While in the governor position, he acts as one of the loudest, most hard-line voices in asserting Argentina's claim. Here he posted about his displeasure at the UK appointing a former executive from an oil company that operated the Malvenas waters as an ambassador to Argentina. Here he described a visit by the UK's ambassador to Chile to the islands as provocative, and here he did the same with former British Prime Minister David Cameron declaring him persona non grata in the province. And the province's Malvenas advocacy goes beyond just words and into action. Sometimes they ban ships flying the British flag from docking at their ports and even blocked cruise ships from visiting if they included the Malvenas on their itinerary. They also approved this provincial flag where this part represents Tirodofuego, the white part represents the Albatross and the five stars represent the disputed islands, including the Malvenas. They declared a song called the March of the Malvenas as the official anthem of the entire province, even though the song only focuses on the oppression of British rule of the Falklands, mandating that it's performed alongside the national anthem at all official events. One of the lines of that song translates to, no land is more wanted than the fatherland as a whole. This snippet is printed on the entire country's 50 peso note whose design fully focuses on the Malvenas. In addition to a map of the islands and another instance of the bicontinental map, the flip side of the note includes a depiction of a goucho, essentially a cowboy, by the name of Antonio Rivera. Who he was and what he did is one of the greatest instances of divergent interpretations of history between Argentina and Britain. Leading up to his era, the British were the first butto to start a settlement on the Falklands. It was called Port Egmont, but it was abandoned just years later as the country faced economic pressure in part due to the American Revolution. As they departed, the British left a plaque asserting their territorial claim. Three decades later, after a moment of Spanish control, the islands lay empty, and Argentina was now independent of its colonial masters. The new country believed it had rights to the Malvenas, largely based on it being the successor to the Spanish territory that itself purported to include the islands. The Spanish claim was based on a combination of first discovery, although that's disputed, and broad territorial divisions between the Spanish and Portuguese empires. Regardless, by this time, Argentina was fully aware the islands existed and believed they were theirs. The exact details of what happened next are also at times disputed, but more or less an Argentinian privateer ended up in the Malvenas, either by order or by chance, and issued some sort of formal declaration of the country's claim. This forms part of Argentina's modern-day claim since the islands were otherwise uninhabited at the time, and many scholars find a mere plaque a dubious assertion of sovereignty by the British. What Argentina did not do, however, was set up a formal civilian settlement. Rather, they permitted various attempts by business people to set up privately funded commercial ventures on the islands, mostly focused on rounding up the feral cattle. The first successful venture came six years later, led by Louis Verne. Verne did not have strong allegiances to any particular nation. He was born in Germany, but lived in Argentina prior to this mission. Aware of the territorial ambiguity, he had gained approval from both Argentina and Britain to go to the islands, and reportedly indicated to the British that he would welcome an assertion of sovereignty by them, but despite this, Argentina named him Governor of the islands, likely to assert their sovereignty through demonstrating a formal government presence. As part of his venture, Verne brought along Argentinian Goucho Antonio Rivera, the figure from the 50 Peso Note. Rivera continued to work the cattle after Verne left and appointed an agent, Matthew Brisbane, and the business continued to operate after British forces came and reasserted their control of the islands in 1833. But here's where history really diverges. In Argentina, particularly in the era leading up to the war, this story of Goucho Rivera was one of a rebel patriot. They say that following the British invasion, he gathered a group of his fellow Gouchos and rebelled against the oppressors, killing five, seizing control of the islands' main building, tearing down the British flag, and replacing it with a makeshift Argentinian one. He was then arrested by British authorities as they recaptured the islands months later, brought to Britain, but even the British courts didn't recognize their authority over the islands, believing them to be outside their jurisdiction, and therefore they deported Goucho Rivera to Brazil, a free man. This formal historians, even in Argentina, now recognize that that story is more legend than legitimate. Their consensus is that Rivera and his fellow rebels were not motivated by patriotism, rather they were just disgruntled employees. Their new boss was paying them in devalued promissary notes that the local shop no longer accepted. They were prevented from returning to Buenos Aires, their workload was becoming unreasonable, they were just mad at their boss, so they murdered him, the shopkeeper that refused their notes, and their allies. As for the legal side, well, it's true that the rebels were released by the British judicial system, but it wasn't that the courts didn't believe Britain held control of the Falklands, rather it was that the courts in England didn't have jurisdiction of the Falklands. In the British legal system, colonies had their own semi-autonomous government with a local judicial system, but that hadn't yet been set up in the islands. After all, it hadn't even been a year since they reasserted their sovereignty. Lackangir's jurisdiction, the courts therefore determined that they couldn't hold the trial. In the century that followed, the British maintained control of the Falklands and grew the population from dozens to hundreds to thousands of predominantly ethnically British settlers. And while the two sides never relinquished their claims, tensions remained relatively low. Each side occasionally offered the other opportunity to negotiate, but they never came to terms and hardly any real action occurred. But in the aftermath of World War II, there was a global push for decolonization, leading to Britain relinquishing much of its overseas territory, and Argentina seemed to perceive this as an opportunity. Rhetoric and pressure ramped up, and during the 1970s, the two sides entered into more regular, more serious negotiations to attempt to rectify their differences. While there were plenty of ideas thrown around, the most momentum centered around a concept mimicking that of Hong Kong, a formal transfer of sovereignty to Argentina alongside a 99-year lease deal back to Britain. The thought was that this would give generations of notice to the islanders to calibrate their lives accordingly. Britain appeared open to this, as there was a growing consensus that the Fockelens dispute was a nuisance. While they were confident in their position, the practical diplomatic and economic negatives outweighed the benefits of continued control of the islands. Argentina, meanwhile, was taken over by a military dictatorship in 1976, and these new leaders started to form a partially correct but ultimately catastrophic opinion. Britain really did not care about the Fockelens. To test their theory, they sent a small squat to the island of Thule in the also disputed South Sandwich Islands, and they set up a base. It took months for Britain to even realize what they'd done, essentially from the British point of view, an invasion of their sovereign territory, but Britain hardly responded. They lodged diplomatic protests, but for the most part left the Argentinian base alone. Another signal came from a series of defense cuts made by the Fatcher government in 1981. They decided to decommission the HMS Endurance, the only royal navy vessel dedicated to South Atlantic patrol and therefore Fockelens defense. This acted as another signal of British apathy to the Argentinians, and they therefore concluded that Britain likely would not resist an Argentinian military invasion, which just wasn't worth it. This, of course, was wrong. Argentina successfully invaded and occupied the islands on April 2, 1982, after a brief resistance by the limited British military presence. In the weeks that followed, change came quick. They set up a rudimentary government, painted over signs with new Spanish names, mandated a driver's to switch to right side driving, deported influential critics, and generally treated the islanders more as prisoners than civilians. Over in London, the Fatcher government quickly ordered royal navy ships to sail south, and through time, this Armada grew to over 100 vessels carrying tens of thousands of personnel. About six weeks after Argentina's landing, British troops set foot on the Fockelens again, and what followed was three and a half bloody weeks of battle, almost a thousand troops died, predominantly from the losing Argentinian side. Back in Britain, Argentina's invasion led to the opposite of what the country hoped. In the years leading up to it, few Britons knew much of anything about the islands. Those that did were aware of them dispassionately. They were just one of many far-flung colonial relics. But immediately after the invasion, there was overwhelming public and government support for military action. In fact, the support was so great that it transformed Margaret Thatcher's political prospects. Prior to the war, her approval rating dipped below 30%, but after its success, it climbed into positive territory above 50%. There's every chance she would not have been reelected in 1983 without this upswell in support, and without that, it's hard to believe she would have been elected again in 1987. So that's to say, without Argentina's invasion, Thatcher would hardly have been able to build the divisive and massively influential legacy that she did. It's at times hard to understand why Britain cares so much about the Fauclens. There's not much strategic value. After all, they themselves are the source of much of the conflict in the region. There's also not a ton of economic value. While there is some oil, so far it's only been drilled in an exploratory manner rather than fully commercial. One of the feelings could stem from Britain's identity as the former foremost global power. The British Empire was the largest in the history of the world, encompassing a full quarter of the global population. That age is long gone. Through decolonization, it steadily lost the vast majority of overseas territory largely since, in almost every case, there was a local population who had the right to self-determination, and most determined that they did not want to be British. The Fauclens were different. There wasn't an indigenous population that was displaced, oppressed, or destroyed. They were just empty islands, and the society that grew was largely of ethnically British people who always identified as British. Therefore, the strength of Britain's claim to the Fauclens was and remains far stronger than that to much of the rest of the empire, even if it's not 100% airtight. Fauclens Islanders did vote on the issue in 2013, and 99.8% elected to remain British, so at least under the principle of self-determination, the claim is strong. Still to this day, the remnants of the British Empire continue to erode. As recently as May 2025, it shrunk yet further with the transfer of sovereignty of the Chagos islands to Mauritius. The legacy here was different. There was a similarly messy legal claim to the territory, but international pressure was much more significant since the UK did displace thousands of islanders to build a joint military facility with the US. So the fact that with the Fauclens Britain believes it has full unambiguous rights, perhaps strengthens their resolve to hold on as they're simultaneously pressured elsewhere. But of course, as the history leading up to the war proves, Britain didn't always care about the Fauclens the way they do today. What changed is that British soldiers spilled blood over the islands. The Philippines lost their fathers and brothers and sons, and the war was recent enough that the whole in those families still loom large. To soften their stamps in the Fauclens would be to diminish the sacrifice of these soldiers. To a British politician, therefore, there's plenty of disincentive to softening their stands on the Fauclens. Across the political spectrum, from labor to lived-dem to conservative, there's striking consensus on the issue, even if there's very much not with other territorial issues relating to Britain's colonial legacy. So Argentina's mistake, perhaps, was the invasion. Without it, there's every chance they would have reached a diplomatic compromise, but now there's little international pressure for the country's to negotiate, and beyond the hundreds of millions of dollars in spending to support the islands, there's little incentive for Britain to change their stance. In Argentina, current willingness to press the issue appears relatively low, but that could change at any moment. The truth, on both sides, is that the average Argentinian or Britain doesn't really care that much about the islands, but they can be made to care. The islands have always proved a useful tool to drum up powerful nationalist sentiments, so whenever that next proves useful for politicians on either side, that's when the islands will gain relevancy once again. Clearly learning about the Fauclens doesn't have the most relevancy to day-to-day life but that tells me that you're likely the type of person who genuinely enjoys learning. You like leveling up your knowledge because even if something is not immediately applicable, having background knowledge always pays off in the long term. That's exactly my thinking with STEM subjects. Even though they're not directly applicable to my day-to-day job or life, I still find it really useful to have that baseline knowledge and that's why I'm such a fan of our sponsor, Brilliant. There's a STEM learning platform that has these masterfully designed courses. I know they're masterfully designed because I massively struggle with math in school, and yet I totally understand things that I absolutely did not previously when learning on Brilliant. They break their subjects down into small little chunks and teach fundamental intuitive principles before getting into the larger topics so you don't just memorize how to do things, you truly understand them. The way you learn is primarily through interactive problem solving, so there's not wrote memorization, it's hands-on learning. In fact, this is proven to be six times more effective than watching videos of lectures, for example. I especially appreciate how Brilliant makes it practical to fit learning into your everyday life, which inevitably is busy. You don't necessarily have time to sit down on the computer and watch an hour-long lecture, but you do have five or ten minutes waiting for the bus when you can knock out one exercise on your phone. If you do that enough, you can start to understand subjects that you might have thought you never would, like calculus or digital circuits or how AI works. With just a small daily commitment, you can totally transform your understanding of these fundamental STEM subjects, and you'll probably learn to love that process of daily learning, waking up each day slightly smarter than the last. You can even test it out without much commitment since if you head over to brilliant.org slash wind-over, scan the QR code on screen, or click the link in the description, you can start learning for free. Tonight, robot umpires officially come to Major League Baseball. Here's how it works. A player thinks the ump blew a ball or strike call. He taps his helmet and 12 cameras review the pitch. Within seconds, the video board shows whether the umpire got it right. The ump doesn't have to review anything. He just announces what the machine decided. And the case for this kind of technology is strong. Umpires blow about 17 or 18 calls a game on average. Earlier this month, a bad strike call knocked the Dominican Republic out of the World Baseball Classic. This new system is accurate to a quarter of an inch. So, calls like that just shouldn't happen anymore. Theoretically, everyone should be happy. But, here's the thing. Once you've said the machine is the final authority on whether a call is right, which is exactly what baseball just did, you've quietly eliminated the reason for having a human there at all. In the minors, where they have tested this for years, umpires have already started calling pitches the way they thought the robot would. Meaning, the human stops being the expert and becomes a rough draft for the AI. And we've seen this before. Tennis introduced the same kind of challenge system in 2006. It was supposed to be a compromise, but [music] by 2025, Wimbledon had eliminated the human line judges entirely. The challenge system wasn't the destination. It was a stop on the road to full automation. The question for baseball isn't whether machines will eventually call balls and strikes. It's how long we can expect humans to keep their positions before they're completely replaced. And that's not just a question for baseball. Read about this in the Future Perfect newsletter at vox.com. You can find the link in our bio.

You are a viral short-form video creator coach with deep expertise in YouTube Shorts storytelling, analytics, video feedback, and content strategy. You speak from real creator experience — direct, no fluff, data-backed.
What are the most effective hook formulas for short-form video in 2025?

From OpusClip research (analyzing thousands of viral Shorts), VidCon 2025, and top creator data:

**Single hook formulas:**
1. **Bold claim**: "You are editing Reels wrong. Fix it in 10 seconds." — direct, slightly confrontational
2. **Question hook**: "Can a $5 tool double your views?" — curiosity gap, low stakes
3. **Curiosity gap**: "This trick gets more saves than hashtags." — implies secret knowledge
4. **Direct promise**: "I'm going to show you X in Y seconds" — sets expectation + mechanism
5. **Contradiction**: "Charging your phone like this makes it slower." — wrong answer first, then flip
6. **POV immersive**: "POV you're about to quit your job…" — drops viewer into the story
7. **Before-you-scroll urgency**: "Before you scroll, watch what happens at the end."
8. **Personal stakes**: "My kitchen is broken and I need to fix it by cooking for strangers." — ironic personal why

**Layered hooks (most viral):** Combine visual pattern interrupt + question, OR contradiction + promise.
- "Longer Shorts actually get more views, and I'll prove it in 30 seconds." (contradiction + timed promise)
- Visual of unexpected thing + "Here's why [common wisdom] is completely wrong."

**Avoid:**
- Burying the lede (most interesting point 15+ seconds in = 70% already swiped)
- Clickbait mismatch (kills long-term performance even if it works short-term)
- Starting with "hey guys" or any non-value opener
- "Let's get started" transitions — kills pace

**Hook length:** 3 seconds max for the hook itself. Jenny Hoyos: hook + foreshadow combined = 2 lines, under 3 seconds.

Hurricane Katrina was an entirely expected storm. It was certainly a bad category 5 hurricane, but it was nowhere near the worst. Plenty more powerful storms have hit the mainland US since 2005, yet none came even close to the degree of human tragedy that Katrina unleashed. The reason this one became perhaps the most remembered modern US hurricane, even two decades on, has little to do with the weather itself. Rather, it's because the truest disaster was the response. In the days immediately before and after the storm, a series of operational failures unfolded, and the consequence was dozens, hundreds, perhaps even more than a thousand, preventable deaths. The earliest stages went as they should have. The US's weather agencies tracked the storm from the earliest phases of its formation, and as it rapidly intensified and its severity became clear about 48 hours out from landfall, the Federal Emergency Management Agency, or FEMA, initiated level 1 disaster operations at its headquarters, as did its two regional offices whose territory lay in the storm's path. From here on, all three offices would be staffed and working 24-7, while all 15 of the emergency support functions at their disposal would now be activated. Importantly, as the agencies aligned with these emergency support functions allude to, while FEMA's often assumed to be the federal arms solely responsible for disaster aid and relief, it's better understood as the agency tasked with coordinating response and aid. For help with major infrastructure problems, for instance, it'll coordinate with the Army Corps of Engineers, for firefighting, the Department of Agriculture, which houses the US Forest Service. Rather than run the show itself, it's the role of FEMA to listen to what local and state-level emergency offices need, then coordinate with other federal offices and the private sector alike to procure the physical goods or expertise required in the disaster-stricken area. But while not its primary intention, FEMA does still have some resources of its own, and these had been mobilized by noon on Saturday still two days out from landfall. The Region 6 office mobilized one of the agency's five mobile emergency response support units or murs to Camp Boergaard, Louisiana. On our moda of 275 vehicles carrying massive power generators, heating cooling systems, various telecommunication systems, multiple thousand gallon tankers of fuel and water and water purification systems, murs units represent the brunt of FEMA's on-the-ground capabilities. With each murs unit is also a mobile emergency operation vehicle, a truck-controller configuration capable of serving as a mobile headquarters and communications aid, and in this unit, goes by the name of Red October. In addition to the murs unit, FEMA staged nearly 550,000 liters of water, 700,000 pounds of ice, and 330,000 meals ready to eat at the camp, along with other supplies positioned elsewhere around the region. The day before landfall, the map of supplies and personnel deployed at the federal level looked like this, with an emergency response team member being appointed federal coordinating officer here in Baton Rouge, where federal response would coordinate with the state of Louisiana's office of emergency preparedness. The storm seemed massive. Experts believe that if it lived up to its billing that the levees surrounding New Orleans were likely to fail, but the response appeared coordinated and ready. But as Katrina barrelled towards the coast, signs of the response's potential limitations began to flicker. On account of high winds, not all water and food sent to supply the superdome, the major designated shelter, made it to the location, further limiting already constrained supplies. And some disagreement between New Orleans' mayor Ray Negan and FEMA head Michael Brown had already bubbled up on how to use the superdome to begin with, as the latter argued that it shouldn't be a shelter of last resort since engineering reports showed the structure may not even hold in the event of a category 3 hurricane. A third party seemed to draw similar conclusion, as the American Red Cross deemed the stadium too dangerous and refused to stage their own staff there during the storm, announcing they'd provide resources to the shelter only after it had passed. Part of the hesitation stemmed from the concern that opening up the facility could encourage people to stay in the city rather than evacuate, and in the days and hours when these hurried conversations were going on, the primary focus was to get as many people out of the city as possible. New Orleans had a specific evacuation plan that called for the southernmost parishes to leave first. These, Lafouche, Plaquemaine, St. Bernard, and St. Charles, were notified to start evacuations on Saturday at 9 a.m. by their parish leaders. That afternoon, Sheriff's deputies were managing the intersections and traffic lights were turned off to ex-bite traffic out of town. By Sunday, deputies were going door to door to tell anyone left to leave. With a nearly 98% evacuation rate and ultimately only 3 deaths in the Plaquemaine community, this strategy worked, but not all areas were successful. The next parishes, lower Orleans, upper Orleans, and Jefferson parishes were also supposed to evacuate by implementing a contra-flow traffic strategy which meant turning all lanes of New Orleans highways into one-way roads directed out of the city, thus increasing capacity. In the past, these evacuations were only, quote, recommended. In fact, different parishes used different wording, but none of them conveyed the urgency and no one made the explicit call that residents actually needed to get out. By the time that Mayor Nagan gave the direction at 11 a.m. on Sunday, less than a day out from landfall that residents of New Orleans really did need to get out and they needed to get out now, there were an estimated 70,000 people still in the city. Besides intense gridlock due to sheer numbers, the problem now was that many of the tens of thousands of the remaining residents had no way of actually leaving. They lacked transportation or were either disabled or elderly and couldn't travel alone. Officials offered some solutions. They asked these remaining residents to meet at coordinated pickup zones where buses would pick them up, but these buses only transported these vulnerable populations as far as the Superdome were an estimated 12,000 people were sheltered by Sunday evening. In the early hours of Monday, August 29th, Hurricane Katrina did what forecasters expected it to. It smashed into the Gulf Coast with 25 foot waves and 130 mile per hour winds. And while New Orleans dodged the brunt of the storm's direct impacts, the surge of water that followed became the critical problem. Unsubstantiated reports of levy issues began rolling in in the morning, then at 6 p.m. as the skies began to clear, a coast guard flyover with a FEMA official aboard verified as much. The fragile levy's task with keeping water out along the lowline north side of the city had failed as engineers had feared they would. As the storm gave way, flood water poured into the city and the race was now on to get supplies and responders in and to get the stranded out. And immediately, a massive problem. Flood water had reached this building and the rising water destroyed its emergency generator. This building was home to Mayor Nagan's office and served as New Orleans City Hall housing the city's emergency operation center or EOC. Today, in a post-Cotrina reality, New Orleans has the ability to take its emergency operation center on the move in the event that the EOC office is compromised, but that wasn't the case in 2005, so the office and mayor had to relocate here to the high-regency where the office would be stationed for the following days. Still, communications were dead. One phone line from the mayor's room would occasionally connect an outbound call, but could not receive them. Situation for email. Not until three days after landfall with the Command Center be able to make calls or receive emails. This created a massive command and control problem, turning a large but expected natural disaster into an utter debacle. Formal National Scale disaster relief efforts in the US are structured as a federalist system that begins with and relies on local governments to pull resources from state and federal levels. The larger governments don't proactively push resources in, they technically wait for the smaller local governments to ask for them. FEMA, the National Guard under the purview of the Department of Defense, the State of Louisiana even, were thus all waiting to hear exactly what supplies mayor Nagan and the city's Command Center needed and where. But with cell towers down, landlines flooded and the internet cut off, the city couldn't get information out. Of course, it didn't help that New Orleans Parish officials had not adopted satellite phone backups in the years prior, nor did it help that federal planners hadn't positioned enough communication backups near enough to the city. Recognizing the problem at hand, that pull logistics would not be possible without being able to connect the joint field office in Baton Rouge to city officials, FEMA attempted to push communications capabilities into the city. The day following landfall, Merse vehicles, including red October, were mobilized from Barxdale Air Force Base and headed to the city core to establish comms. There they would meet a FEMA coordinator being flown in by helicopter to form a unified command, oversee commodities ordered, and build out an operational base for search and rescue. While the coordinator made it to the Superdome, there was little he could do but wait for the communications vehicles, which by nightfall, had only made it as far as Lafayette as navigating debris made progress slow. The next day, it became clear they wouldn't make it at all as floodwaters were too high for the vehicles to make it to the city core, turning red October back to Baton Rouge. They had gotten tantalizingly close, as one Merse vehicle made it as far as an overpass within view of the Superdome, but no farther. Two days after landfall, as the city went from under siege by a storm and back to miserably hot summer, FEMA had yet to establish stable communications between the local emergency officials and Baton Rouge, or state and federal resources waited for direction. While the lack of preposition communication resources within the city would be criticized in the months and years to follow, for now this meant major chaos in getting needed resources in. For the thousands upon thousands of pounds of commodities palatized in positions around New Orleans, food, water, ice, tarps, generators, disaster planners had designed a system where requests would be made by city and parish operation centers, they'd carefully be input by the state, and they'd be executed and tracked by FEMA through its database. But now commodities were being requested via word of mouth and over the radio by overwhelmed parish officials, they were being hastily input at the state operation center without any vetting and half hazard filling, and they were reaching, or not reaching, FEMA, unformatted, unverified, and often just incorrect. A logistics chain carefully designed to procure and track resources, but built around the assumed reliability of phone lines and internet access, had now turned into a stress-charged game of telephone. As goods did begin to make their way into the city, they were the wrong goods or the wrong amount of the right goods. In some cases, the request they swore they made just never showed up at all, and without much of a real coherent paper trail, no one knew exactly why they didn't. Without communications, logistics failed at all levels. Localities didn't get what they needed, or were too overwhelmed to even make the request, while FEMA sent truck drivers hauling ice on 4,000 miles or 6,500 km wild goose hunts at the struggle to figure out where to store, then distribute goods on the fly. All the while, as federal, state, and local emergency operators worked to scramble goods into the city, FEMA slowly began to mobilize to get people out. In the years prior, New Orleans officials had done significant amounts of planning on evacuating people from the city before the big one hit, but less thought had actually been given to getting people out after a major weather event, and this had disastrous consequences. One of these unforeseen challenges occurred in what was supposed to be the main evacuation space for refugees, Superdome. Up to 12,000 people made it inside pre-Lanfall, but on Monday morning, parts of the arenas roof tore open, compromising both power and water infrastructure in the facility. The predictions of structural deficiency proved correct. By Tuesday, the day after Lanfall, the shelter was providing little shelter. Its supplies were dwindling, its lower levels were flooding, its safety and level of sanitation were dropping. It would need to be evacuated as soon as possible, and yet people were still arriving in a horde from now sunken parts of the city. By Wednesday, the population of the Superdome swelled to 23,000. As time drew on, the safe haven of last resort was proving anything but safe. The electricity system failed, and the backup generator could only power the internal lighting. The field level flooded, the plumbing failed, and their refrigeration systems went out, and food started to quickly spoil in the heat and humidity. Media reports were alarming and tension was increasing, but because it took until early Wednesday morning for FEMA to officially assign the U.S. Department of Transportation, the task of mobilizing buses nationwide to assist in evacuating the Superdome, getting people out was going to take a while. Because much of the region had been devastated, the plan was to evacuate those in the Superdome to the Houston Astrodome, some 350 miles or 560 kilometers away. But the city's school buses that had been staged to evacuate people before the storm were now underwater, so officials scrambled to find buses across the state, along with drivers willing to take on the task, which seemed increasingly dangerous as overblown or unsubstantiated reports of violence began to permeate from the city. By that evening, nearly two days after landfall, bus evacuations finally began, but it was hardly a major mobilization as a meager 120 evacuees headed towards Houston. According to Makeup for Lost Time, emergency contracts with bus companies as far as New York were set up, while the governor passed an executive order commandeering school buses, but evacuations stopped and started in fits and starts as rumors of sniper fire and other violent screw louder, thanks to faulty reporting by national media. Now, for a bit of context, my wife keeps insisting that my best friend was somehow associated with a time, which was before we were married and not living together, when I had a brief fling that she did not approve of. This was with a young woman who did certain type of work, if you know what I mean. And I have to say also, this was at a time in my life when I too was making some really poor choices. But I also very quickly came to my senses, I got it out of my system, and I moved on. I also never kept it a secret, and I thought my now wife had moved past all of that since I believe that I had made amends years and years ago, but evidently she does not think so. She's been bringing all of this up again that literally happened 18 years ago, trying to paint me as some kind of criminal. And here's the thing, she also thinks that somehow my best friend met this person, and furthermore insists that mine and my best friend's relationship is not based on anything healthy. She's also removed my credit cards from my wallet, most of which are paid off, and insists that I'm not allowed to book this trip. She says it's too much of an expense, and that she forbids me from going. I should also mention that none of these cards are in her name, since she has no credit and no job, whereas I make a fairly decent living in TV broadcasting, while also doing Lyft on the side to earn some extra money, which totals out to like over a hundred thousand dollars a year. Meanwhile, she hasn't had a full-time job in almost five years. I pay for everything, including her health insurance, and have actually reined in a lot of my previous debts. And sure, I absolutely have made mistakes, like I'm not a perfect person, but I've owned up to things and I moved on. And I really thought we mended things from the past, at least in the sense that she married me knowing all that we had been through. But she also forgets that much of what happened occurred while we were living apart. She confuses timelines to insist that I was cheating, when in actuality I was doing stuff that she didn't like, fairly openly mind you, as a single male with other consenting adults. And I definitely cheated before we were married and caused breakups, but it was maybe once when I was fully in the throes of being an addict. And 90% of the supposed incidents that she recalls were definitely attempts to cheat that just failed and went sideways. But with all that in mind, I've never cheated in our marriage, and I thought all of that was behind us, but my god, I guess not. I'm so livid right now. I feel like she has no right to tell me whether I can undertake this, and that she is way off base for even judging the basis of my relationship with a person I consider to be like a brother to me. So, am I overreacting right now? Because honestly, I don't know what to do.

So, most people have heard of the places of Tahiti and Bora Bora, but they don't even know like what or where these things are. They're just like, "OH MY GOD, LOOK SO PRETTY. I JUST WANT TO GO there for my honeymoon." Well, if that ignorant [Â __Â ] mentality is you or somebody you know, send them this video cuz I'm going to give you an overview. This right here is the big ass Pacific Ocean. As you can see, no continental land masses, just a bunch of small islands. And in the Pacific Ocean, Tahiti and Bora Bora are located in this island cluster right here. This is French Polynesia. It's located about 2200 miles or 3,500 km south of Hawaii. As the name implies, it is a French overseas collectivity that is administered by France. However, they have a high degree of autonomy. They basically have self-ruule, so they can kind of manage things like their own taxation, budget, language, education, infrastructure, and labor laws. They even have their own currency. They can even sign international agreements and join IGOs as long as they notify France. Whereas France just steps in for things like defense, military, foreign policy, and justice. French Polynesia is divided into five administrative divisions with six island chains. Each cluster has their own Polynesian language. However, French and Tahesian are used as lingua frankas between them. So, let's summarize each island chain, shall we? So, these are the society islands divided into leeward and winward. These are the island chains that have Tahiti and Bora Bora, the most famous islands in the entire collectivity. You've seen those pictures of like the lagoon with the bungalows. Yeah, that's Bora Bora. And Tahiti is the biggest of all the islands. About a third of the entire population lives in these islands. And this is like the heart and center of Tahesian/ Eastern Polynesian culture. You've probably been exposed to Tahesian culture at some point in media. Like their dance is considered one of the most difficult ones to master in the world because it takes an insane amount of precise movements with uncomfortable positions. Next up, the Marces. This is kind of like the warrior islands. Like these people have a lot more tattoos and stone platforms and carvings. Their islands don't have a lot of reefs or lagoons, so they focus a lot more on like landbased activities and they eat a lot more pigs. In that regard, the Tuamotu peoples live mostly on flat low-lying atoles with no freshwater sources. So they had to become masters of like sailing and fishing and they had to use like rain water and coconuts for hydration. Now the Gambier or Gambia Islands are collectively joined with Touamu as a single administrative division, but they kind of have their own distinct unique culture. Like out of all the island chains, they were the only ones that actually had like a kingdom lineagebased society ruled by a king known as an Ariki Nui. Whereas all the other island chains had a fluid chieftain system. Also, they are known for being the pearl people. They were the masters of pearl farming. And finally, the Australas. This is the coolest temperate zone of French Polynesia with seasons more similar to New Zealand. In fact, the people who are actually more culturally and linguistically related to the Mauy people of New Zealand as opposed to Tahesians. And these people were a lot more focused on land-based agriculture. So, they ate a lot more things like taro and cabbages and whatever. They ate their veggies. So, yeah. I mean, Bora Bor and Tahiti aren't the only ones. It's like there's 118 of these islands. I mean, why confine yourself to just two? Hop around and check out the others. Cheers.

Today we're going to look at one of my favorite authors, Leanne Morardi's book, Big Little Lies, and we're going to fix the first page. Right here in the first sentence, the author opens two loops. Who is Marie Antinet? That's kind of an odd name. And then we ask this question that sounds like a riot. And so now we want to know, who is Marie Antinet? And what is this riot that's taking place that hooks us immediately? Now, she immediately pays off Marie Antonette when we find out it's a cat. But then this open loop doesn't pay off until all the way down here when we find out there's angry hollers crash through the quiet cold night air. This is one of my favorite things about Lean Morardi's writing is her ability to constantly keep open loops that keeps the reader hooked. She also does a good job with her verb and adjective and adverb selection to make it very specific. Again, specificity breeds universality for readers. So, she wasn't just watching television. the character was watching Dancing with the Stars. And finally, we often wonder what's good showing and what's good telling. Almost all of your writing should be showing. This whole page is showing, but this one line of telling is a good example of what good telling looks like. We find out a lot about this character when Leanne Morardi just tells us that she grew up with an angry mother. So, overall, I would give this first page an A+. It hooks us as the reader. We want to know what's going to happen next.
